title: Babur from Ferghana
subtitle: a refugee Timurid prince conquers India with Ottoman gunpowder tactics
bullets: Babur was a fifth-generation descendant of Timur on his father's side and a Genghisid through his mother's, but he spent his first twenty-one years losing battles in Central Asia and was driven out of his ancestral Ferghana by the Uzbeks in 1504.
He crossed the Hindu Kush and seized Kabul in 1504, which became his base for the next twenty-two years and the operational launching point for the eventual invasion of India.

His Turkish gun-master Ustad Ali Quli had served in the Ottoman army before joining the Timurids and brought the wagon-fort tabur cengi tactic with him — a few hundred matchlock musketeers behind chained baggage carts, with field cannons in the gaps.

At the First Battle of Panipat on April 21, 1526, Babur's force of perhaps 12,000 broke Ibrahim Lodi's army of around 100,000 in a single morning; Ibrahim Lodi was killed, his army was annihilated, and Babur entered Delhi unopposed three days later.

Babur defeated the Rajput coalition under Rana Sanga at the Battle of Khanwa in 1527, securing his foothold in north India, and ruled from Delhi and Agra until his death in December 1530 at age forty-seven.

The Baburnama, his Chagatai-Turkic memoir, is one of the great autobiographies of world literature — describing his campaigns, his alcohol consumption, his depressions, his romantic life, and the precise grades of melon available in different Ferghana villages with the same matter-of-fact candor.

The three great Islamic gunpowder empires — the Ottomans, the Safavids, and the Mughals — were all founded or transformed within a forty-year window, with Babur's victory at Panipat as the final piece of the early-modern Persianate-Islamic political settlement.

title: Humayun's Catastrophe and Recovery
subtitle: the second Mughal emperor loses the dynasty, recovers it, and dies on the stairs
bullets: Humayun inherited an unstable empire and faced immediate threats from his own brothers (who controlled large appanages) and from the rising Afghan general Sher Shah Suri in the east.
Sher Shah Suri defeated Humayun at Chausa in 1539 and Kannauj in 1540, drove him into exile, and ruled north India as the Sur Sultanate for five years before dying in a gunpowder accident.

Humayun spent fifteen years in exile, reaching the Safavid court of Shah Tahmasp in 1544 and accepting humiliating terms — public Shia observance plus the cession of Kandahar — in exchange for a loan of 12,000 Persian troops to recover his throne.
His son Akbar was born in the Sindh desert in 1542 in a tent at a Hindu vassal's outpost while Humayun was on the run from his own brother Kamran.

Humayun finally recaptured Delhi in July 1555, six months before he died on January 24, 1556, by tripping on the steps of his library while carrying an armful of books — the British historian Stanley Lane-Poole later wrote that he stumbled out of life as he had stumbled through it.

The Persian exile seeded Mughal cultural history: Humayun brought back Tabriz painters Mir Sayyid Ali and Abd al-Samad who founded the Mughal painting workshop, plus Persian architects, calligraphers, and the cultural orientation that would define the Mughal court for the next two centuries.

Humayun's Tomb at Delhi, built by his widow Hamida Banu Begum in 1572 and designed by the Persian architect Mirak Mirza Ghiyas, was the first great Mughal garden tomb and the direct architectural template for the Taj Mahal eighty years later.

title: Akbar the Great
subtitle: the illiterate emperor who built the institutional Mughal state
bullets: Akbar took the throne at thirteen in 1556 with Bairam Khan as regent, who defeated the Hindu general Hemu at the Second Battle of Panipat that same year — the last serious Hindu political claim to Delhi for three centuries.

Akbar was illiterate (the historians are confident on this point) but ran one of the most sophisticated state administrations in the early-modern world, reigning for forty-nine years and expanding the empire across most of the subcontinent.

His Rajput marriage alliance — beginning with the Kachhwaha princess of Amber in 1562 — integrated the most formidable Hindu military power in north India into the Mughal nobility, transforming his most dangerous potential opponents into his most reliable lieutenants.
He abolished the jizya tax on non-Muslim subjects in 1564, the institutional core of the hundred-fifteen-year Hindu-Muslim accommodation that would last until Aurangzeb reimposed the tax in 1679.

Fatehpur Sikri, built 1571–1585 next to a Sufi shrine, was a complete planned royal city blending Hindu and Islamic architecture in red sandstone — and was abandoned after fourteen years because the water supply failed.

Din-i-Ilahi, the syncretic religion Akbar promulgated in 1582, drew from Islam, Hinduism, Christianity, Zoroastrianism, and Jainism, but never had more than thirty initiated members and disappeared at his death.

The mansabdari system formalized in 1577 assigned every senior Mughal officer a numerical rank from 10 to 10,000 specifying both salary and required cavalry, with non-hereditary jagir land assignments preventing the formation of regional landed aristocracies.

Court historian Abu'l Fazl produced the Akbarnama and the Ain-i-Akbari — the most detailed surviving primary account of any pre-modern Asian state administration, including detailed prices, wages, and revenue statistics still cited by modern economic historians.

Akbar and Elizabeth I of England were almost exact contemporaries; Akbar's empire had revenue roughly thirty times Elizabeth's, and India was by every measure of pre-modern wealth the dominant economy of the early-modern world at the moment of Akbar's death.

title: Jahangir and Nur Jahan
subtitle: the wine-and-opium emperor whose Persian wife actually ran the empire
bullets: Jahangir was, by every primary source that mentions him, an alcoholic and opium addict whose Tuzuk-i-Jahangiri memoir describes his progressive incapacity for the actual work of governance with a candor that has no parallel in seventeenth-century royal autobiography.

Nur Jahan, his Persian-born twentieth wife (formerly Mehr-un-Nissa), ran the empire in his name from about 1614 onward — issuing imperial orders through her own seal, striking coinage in her name, and personally leading an armed elephant-back rescue attempt when Jahangir was kidnapped in 1626.
She was the only Mughal empress ever to wield this kind of executive power, and the English ambassador Sir Thomas Roe wrote home that she governs him and winds him up at her pleasure.

Mughal painting reached its absolute peak under Jahangir's patronage: court masters Mansur (animal and bird studies), Bishan Das (portraiture), and Abu'l Hasan produced naturalistic compositions including the most accurate surviving image of a living dodo.

Mughal painters absorbed European technique from Jesuit-imported prints, producing a unique synthesis of Persian color and European modeling that exists in no other Asian or Middle Eastern painting tradition.

Sir Thomas Roe spent three years at the Mughal court (1615–18) as the first formal English ambassador, securing commercial privileges for the East India Company that would, a hundred and forty years later, become the institutional foundation for Plassey.

The succession struggle of the late reign pitted Jahangir's son Khurram (the future Shah Jahan) against Nur Jahan's preferred candidate Shahryar; Asaf Khan betrayed his sister, secured the throne for Khurram, and Nur Jahan retired to Lahore to write Persian poetry for the next eighteen years.

title: Shah Jahan and the Peacock Throne
subtitle: the Taj Mahal, the Red Fort, and the empire at peak architectural extravagance
bullets: Mumtaz Mahal, Shah Jahan's chief consort and Nur Jahan's niece, died in 1631 at Burhanpur giving birth to her fourteenth child in nineteen years — the sheer reproductive toll directly contributed to her death, and Shah Jahan went into formal mourning for two years.

The Taj Mahal was built between 1632 and 1653 by chief architect Ustad Ahmad Lahori, with white marble from Makrana in Rajasthan and inlaid stones from across Asia — twenty-eight different types of stone in the pietra dura inlay alone.

The Italian-architect myth crediting Geronimo Veroneo with the Taj's design was demolished by modern scholarship; Veroneo was a jeweler, not an architect, and the Mughal court records name Ustad Ahmad Lahori as the chief designer.

Every architectural decision at the Taj was purposeful: the minarets lean three degrees outward to fall away from the main building in earthquakes, the dome is double-shelled for proportional balance, and the calligraphic inscriptions are subtly enlarged toward the top so they appear consistent in size from the ground.
Shah Jahan moved the capital from Agra to a new walled city at Delhi called Shahjahanabad in 1648, anchored by the Red Fort palace and the Jama Masjid mosque — Shahjahanabad is now Old Delhi.
The Peacock Throne, completed in 1635 after seven years of construction, was set with the Koh-i-Noor diamond and the Timur Ruby and was reportedly worth twice the cost of the Taj Mahal.

Shah Jahan's reign saw four expensive wars over Kandahar (Mughal in 1638, Safavid in 1649 after Aurangzeb's three failed reconquest attempts) — the strategic city changed hands four times in the seventeenth century without lasting Mughal control.
When Shah Jahan fell ill in September 1657, his four sons by Mumtaz Mahal — Dara Shukoh, Shah Shuja, Aurangzeb, and Murad Bakhsh — immediately began the bloodiest war of succession in Mughal history.

title: Aurangzeb
subtitle: the most controversial Mughal: orthodox Sunni state-builder, religious zealot, or both at once
bullets: Aurangzeb defeated his elder brother Dara Shukoh at the Battle of Samugarh in 1658 — partly because Dara dismounted from his elephant mid-battle and his army mistook the empty howdah for his death — then imprisoned his father Shah Jahan in Agra Fort with a window facing the Taj Mahal.

Dara Shukoh was a Sufi-Hindu syncretist who translated fifty Upanishads into Persian as the Sirr-i-Akbar; his execution in 1659 closed off the Akbarian-syncretist alternative path the Mughal Empire might have taken.

Dara's Persian Upanishads were retranslated into Latin in 1801 and read by Schopenhauer, who called them the consolation of his life — Mughal philosophy reached European thought through the brother Aurangzeb killed.

Aurangzeb reimposed the jizya tax on non-Muslim subjects in 1679 after a 115-year gap since Akbar's abolition, signaling the empire's official return to orthodox Sunni state policy and provoking immediate Rajput rebellions.

Shivaji Bhonsle built the Maratha kingdom in the western Deccan through hill-fort warfare, raided the Mughal port of Surat in 1664 for 100 million rupees in loot, escaped from Mughal custody at Agra in 1666 by hiding in a basket of sweets, and was crowned Chhatrapati at Raigad in 1674.

Aurangzeb annexed the Bijapur Sultanate in 1686 and the Golconda Sultanate in 1687 (Golconda being famous for the diamond mines that produced the Koh-i-Noor), bringing the entire subcontinent under nominal Mughal sovereignty.

He spent his final twenty-six years personally directing the Deccan Wars from age 63 to 88, conducting an unwinnable counter-insurgency against the Maratha hill forts that consumed armies, revenue, and ultimately the institutional cohesion of the empire.

Aurangzeb's reign overlapped almost exactly with Louis XIV of France (1661–1715) — both centralizing absolutist monarchs whose long expensive wars at the empire's periphery exhausted the treasury and produced visible peaks followed by visible collapses.

title: The Long Decline
subtitle: Nader Shah sacks Delhi, the Marathas rise, the regional powers break away
bullets: The post-Aurangzeb succession war (1707–12) set the pattern of brothers killing brothers; the Sayyid Brothers as kingmakers installed and deposed four Mughal emperors between 1713 and 1720, openly treating the dynasty as their political instrument.
Muhammad Shah Rangila reigned for twenty-nine years (1719–48) patronizing music, painting, and poetry while doing almost nothing to address the empire's structural collapse.
The Marathas under Peshwa Baji Rao I expanded out of the western Deccan into central and northern India, raiding Delhi itself in 1737 and forcing Muhammad Shah to accept Maratha territorial demands.

The Nizamate of Hyderabad founded by Asaf Jah I in 1724 became autonomous, the Nawabate of Bengal under Murshid Quli Khan and Alivardi Khan became autonomous, the Awadh Nawabs became autonomous, and the Sikh Khalsa was emerging as a serious military power in the Punjab.

The Persian conqueror Nader Shah Afshar invaded India in 1738–39 in pursuit of Afghan refugees, defeated Muhammad Shah's army of 300,000 at the Battle of Karnal in a single afternoon, and occupied Delhi for fifty-seven days.

Nader Shah's troops killed perhaps 20,000 to 30,000 Delhi residents in reprisals after a riot, looted 700 million rupees in cash and goods, and carried off the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor diamond — Nader was able to suspend Iranian taxation for three years on the proceeds.

Ahmad Shah Durrani's Afghan force destroyed the Maratha army at the Third Battle of Panipat in 1761 — Maratha casualties of perhaps 60,000 to 100,000 temporarily checked their northern expansion and left the power vacuum that the British would shortly fill.

The Black Hole of Calcutta in 1756, when Siraj-ud-Daulah captured the East India Company settlement and prisoners died of suffocation overnight, was substantially exaggerated in British accounts (modern scholars suggest 18 to 43 deaths among 64 prisoners rather than the legendary 123 of 146) but became the political pretext for the Company's conquest of Bengal.

title: The Company Takes Over
subtitle: a chartered British corporation conquers the wealthiest part of the early-modern world
bullets: Robert Clive's force of 3,000 defeated Siraj-ud-Daulah's army of 50,000 at the Battle of Plassey on June 23, 1757 — primarily because Clive had bribed Bengal's senior commander Mir Jafar to keep his troops out of the fighting.

Mir Jafar was installed as the new Nawab of Bengal in exchange for £2.34 million paid to the Company plus £234,000 personally to Clive, and Plassey is conventionally taken as the starting point of British India.

The Battle of Buxar in 1764 destroyed a coalition of the Mughal emperor Shah Alam II, the Nawab of Awadh, and the deposed Mir Qasim of Bengal; the Treaty of Allahabad in 1765 transferred the Diwani right to collect Bengal land revenue from the Mughal emperor to the East India Company.

The Bengal Famine of 1770 killed approximately 10 million people — a third of the Bengal population — partly from drought but substantially exacerbated by Company revenue policies that maintained tax demands as grain prices spiraled.

Tipu Sultan of Mysore pioneered the military use of iron-cased rockets that the British copied as the Congreve rocket — the rockets red glare in the American national anthem refers to British weapons derived directly from Tipu Sultan's Mysore artillery.
Tipu sent embassies to Ottoman Constantinople and revolutionary France attempting to build an anti-British coalition; he was killed defending Seringapatam in May 1799.

Arthur Wellesley (the future Duke of Wellington) defeated the combined Maratha army at the Battle of Assaye in 1803 — Wellington reportedly described Assaye later in life as the toughest battle he ever fought, and the man had Waterloo to compare it to.

The Anglo-Sikh Wars of 1845–49 ended with the British annexation of the Punjab and the surrender of the Koh-i-Noor diamond, which Garrard's of London recut from 186 to 105 carats at Queen Victoria's instruction in 1852 and set into the British crown.

title: The 1857 Rebellion and the End
subtitle: the cartridges, the Delhi siege, and Bahadur Shah Zafar in Rangoon
bullets: The trigger for the rebellion was the Pattern 1853 Enfield rifle cartridges, greased with a rumored mix of cow tallow and pig lard that polluted both Hindu and Muslim soldiers — the Company quickly withdrew the cartridges but the trust damage was already done.

Sepoys mutinied at Meerut on May 10, 1857, marched south through the night to Delhi, and proclaimed the eighty-two-year-old Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar emperor of India on May 11 — over his reluctant initial refusal.

The rebellion spread across north India through May, June, and July 1857, with major centers at Delhi, Lucknow, Cawnpore, Allahabad, and Jhansi, but the Bombay and Madras armies largely did not mutiny and most of the south, west, and far north of India did not rebel.

British and Sikh-Punjabi forces retook Delhi in five days of street fighting in September 1857; Bahadur Shah fled to Humayun's Tomb where he was captured by William Hodson, who personally shot the emperor's three sons in the chest the next day at what is now called Khooni Darwaza, the Bloody Gate.

British reprisals at Delhi continued for months: house-to-house executions, the expulsion of the city's Muslim population, looting of the Red Fort, and the demolition of much of Shahjahanabad — the city's population dropped from about 160,000 in 1857 to perhaps 50,000 in 1861.

Mirza Ghalib, the great Urdu poet of late-Mughal Delhi, lived through the siege and recorded the catastrophe in letters describing the streets empty of people he had known, the houses of friends torn down, the sense of his world ending around him.

Bahadur Shah Zafar was tried for treason in 1858 in the Diwan-i-Khas of the Red Fort, convicted, exiled to Rangoon, and died there in 1862 at age eighty-seven, buried in an unmarked grave to prevent his tomb becoming a pilgrimage site.
The Government of India Act 1858 dissolved the East India Company as a sovereign authority and transferred power to the British Crown; Queen Victoria was styled Empress of India from 1876.

The Mughal inheritance survives in the architecture (the Taj Mahal, the Red Fort, Humayun's Tomb, Fatehpur Sikri), the cuisine (biryanis, kebabs, naan), the Urdu language, the religious geography of the modern subcontinent, and the basic institutional template of the modern Indian and Pakistani states.

The British civilizing mission story has been comprehensively dismantled by modern scholarship: Mughal India in 1757 had a per-capita income comparable to contemporary England, and the conquest extracted wealth on a scale that contributed substantially to British industrialization while reducing Indian per-capita income to perhaps a fifth of British levels by 1947.
